text: Instead of trying to fit imperative code (loses intention details) into a DAG, why not just go full on declarative? I recently finished a declarative-to-imperative code generator which supports your checkpoints, planning on making it a full on visual editor :slightly_smiling_face: <https://twitter.com/LeonardPauli/status/1227010930657169408>

attachments.fallback:
<https://twitter.com/LeonardPauli|@LeonardPauli>: Progress update re the new "medium for thought" I'm building: :white_check_mark: Declarative logic graph to executable imperative code achieved! :star-struck: Next up: extend with web DOM nodes, create visual graph editor graph, use editor to edit editor, delete all the JS! :fire:
<https://youtu.be/jFqGNCNy1s0>

text: Well I'll grant you one downside to not being Duncan — I've already had the one time life experience of some of those big gameplay-narrative revelations. I'll never get to have that first-time again. I'll forever know the realized joy and terror of Outer Wilds, and not feel the looming sense of vast discovery. I will, however, continue to be terrified of falling into a black hole for as long as I live, so at least I have that to look forward to.

text: <@UR482RFPY> I believe <http://runkit.com|runkit.com> also works similar to mathematica, see <http://blog.runkit.com/2015/09/10/time-traveling-in-node-js-notebooks/>

attachments.title: Time Traveling in Node.js Notebooks
attachments.text: As part of a two-post series, I'd like to share some in-depth details behind one of RunKit's most asked about features: time traveling. In this first installment I'll be focusing mainly on how the back-end works: specifically, how we're able to not only rewind the state of your code, but any changes to the filesystem and spawned subprocesses as well. From a high level, this allows for a lot of cool functionality like real undo in a REPL. However, we'll also see how time traveling is actually essential to the way notebooks fundamentally work in RunKit.
attachments.fallback: RunKit Blog: Time Traveling in Node.js Notebooks

text: Really nice! :slightly_smiling_face:  I like that there are three ways to enter a div (or any other tag): a specific div block, a generic block where you choose the tag name in a dropdown and a very open block where you type the tag name. text: > _In this podcast, Daniel Bryant sat down with <https://www.linkedin.com/in/gregthelaw/|Greg Law>, CTO at Undo. Topics discussed included: the challenges with debugging modern software systems, the need for “hyper-observability” and the benefit of being able to record and replay exact application execution; and the challenges with implementing the capture of nondeterministic system data in Undo’s LiveRecorder product for JVM-based languages that are Just-In-Time (JIT) compiled._

text: <@UR482RFPY> in an interview a while back Stephen Wolfram pointed out that Mathematica is free actually, if you use the kids' learning portal; it has a kiddie looking interface but it is full engine. Not only is Mathematica the only big symbolic computation system around (it can produce arbitrary precision if you wish), he has spent the last 10 years building up a database of knowledge about the world, with historical records for a vast number of real things. He has spent hundreds of millions building up databases that can be accessed.

text:
Hey everyone :wave:, I'm Joe. It's great to be here amongst like-minded individuals.

I am the co-founder of Budibase, an open-source low-code platform for building web apps. We're aiming to have a demo ready by the end of the month, which will involve us building a simple CRM. After we complete the demo, we will move into alpha/beta :rocket:. If you would like to discover more about Budibase, and potentially sign up, visit our website using the link below:

Budibase website - <https://www.budibase.com/>

Have a great day/night everyone and if you have any questions, simply reach out to me here or via email: joe at <http://budibase.com|budibase.com>

attachments.title: Budibase: Web App Builder - Build Your Web App With Speed
attachments.text: Budibase is a modern, open source web app builder for creating, launching and growing web applications. Budibase eliminates repetition and dramatically reduces development time. Check it out.

text: <@UF1ATC4RF> the approach of Undo's time traveling debugging is to capture microscopic level of changes. This is a heavy process and would not be normally enabled for a customer running a graphical interactive program. So what would be more desirable is such a lightweight recording technology that it can be enabled at all times, and then if a problem occurs the recording can be transmitted and now you are in an era of 100% repeatable, fixable software. This is the approach i have used in Beads.  But Undo is offering debugging for systems that were never designed for this, which is very clever.

text: I don't know what you mean by Wikidata. The Wolfram|Alpha knowledge engine is what it is called. I am not a user, so i can't comment on the quantity, accuracy and breadth of their data, but i am very familiar with Wolfram's long career and you don't get to be one of the largest language vendor companies on the planet (700 employees) without doing something right. I just tested it, and asked it "temperature records for NYC" and it gave me a fantastic readout. Google doesn't do anything near to this. Google's search engine is based on page ranks, and its purpose is to sell advertising; Alpha is trying to understand human speech, and uses thousands of heuristics. It has evolved considerably over the 10 years, and it is to my knowledge the best knowledge engine for general use; only Watson has a deeper model of the world, and Watson is not available for consumer use.

elements.text: I don't know what you mean by Wikidata. The Wolfram|Alpha knowledge engine is what it is called. I am not a user, so i can't comment on the quantity, accuracy and breadth of their data, but i am very familiar with Wolfram's long career and you don't get to be one of the largest language vendor companies on the planet (700 employees) without doing something right. I just tested it, and asked it "temperature records for NYC" and it gave me a fantastic readout. Google doesn't do anything near to this. Google's search engine is based on page ranks, and its purpose is to sell advertising; Alpha is trying to understand human speech, and uses thousands of heuristics. It has evolved considerably over the 10 years, and it is to my knowledge the best knowledge engine for general use; only Watson has a deeper model of the world, and Watson is not available for consumer use.

text: Wikidata is a semantic/structured database that is editable like Wikipedia. It's a wiki Foundation project. It's built around "triples" of the form (entity relationship entity). For example E123 might be "London", R34 might be "is the capital of" and E25 might be "UK", then one entry would be (E123 R34 E25)

text: in erlang you can turn distributed tracing per module, function or even function with a guard to match specific values on the arguments, then you can log them or ship them somewhere else. No extra stuff needed, always available at runtime. I fixed a really weird bug that I couldn't reproduce by tracing some functions in less than 15 minutes, directly in production

To add to the discussion — I'm very keen on the idea of doing the replay capture within customer software.. but that seems like such a huge divergence from how things currently work. There's privacy concerns, right? There's performance concerns, for sure.

<@UEQ6M68H0>, how do you do this in Beads?
